input.background: Acute lower respiratory tract infection is a leading cause of death among infants and under-5s in developing countries. A simple test is needed to differentiate between infants with acute respiratory infection who have lower respiratory tract infection (LRI) and require antibiotics, and those with upper respiratory tract infection (URI). The study explores the usefulness of respiratory rate as a diagnostic tool for this purpose.
input.data:
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Respiratory rate (breaths/min) & Number of children (\%) \\
\hline & LRI & URI \\
\hline 0-30 & 1 (1\%) & 16 (11\%) \\
\hline 31-40 & 4 (3\%) & 77 (51\%) \\
\hline 41-50 & 10 (7\%) & 46 (30\%) \\
\hline 51-60 & 41 (29\%) & 9 (6\%) \\
\hline 61+ & 86 (61\%) & 3 (2\%) \\
\hline Total & 142 (100\%) & 151 (100\%) \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
input.question: If a respiratory rate of >50 breaths/min is taken as an indication of LRI and all such children are treated with antibiotics, what proportion of treated infants will have been treated unnecessarily? What proportion of LRI infants would not get antibiotics?

output.answer: The PPV at a cut-off of >50 breaths/min is 26%, meaning that 74% of treated infants will have been treated unnecessarily. The sensitivity at this cut-off is 89%, meaning that 89% of LRI infants would receive antibiotics, while 11% would not get antibiotics. At a lower cut-off of 40 breaths/min, the proportion of LRI infants treated with antibiotics would be higher (96%), but only 7% of those treated would have LRI. The choice of cut-off must consider non-statistical factors.
